courseSubtitle: A coordinate and operator framework for quaternionic hypersphere and scale geometry

chapters.title: The Geometry of Numbers
chapters.subtitle: From real to complex to quaternionic
objectives: Understand the progression from real to complex to quaternionic numbers.
Use quaternion basis elements and noncommutativity correctly.
Visualize unit quaternions as points on S^3.
Apply conjugation, norm, and inverse as geometric operations.
checkpointPrompts: Why do quaternions require more than one imaginary direction?
Why is noncommutativity geometrically meaningful?
checkpointAnswers: They preserve scalar plus three oriented imaginary components, so phase can carry direction.
It reflects the fact that rotations and oriented transformations generally depend on order.
chapters.finalTakeaway: The numerical root of QSG is not a replacement for real or complex numbers; it is a way to preserve orientation-sensitive structure.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Standard quaternion background and coordinate setup.

sections.title: Real, complex, and quaternionic planes visualized

chapters.title: The Quaternionic Rotation Form
chapters.subtitle: q = cos(phi) + n-hat sin(phi) and the 3-sphere
objectives: Derive the rotation form q = cos(phi) + n-hat sin(phi).
Distinguish the rotor axis n-hat from the real scale coordinate s.
Connect unit quaternions to SU(2) and SO(3).
Explain the double-cover and 4pi rotation identity.
Read geodesics on S^3 as great-circle rotor motion.
checkpointPrompts: What does n-hat carry in q = cos(phi) + n-hat sin(phi)?
How is n-hat different from s?
Why does SU(2) appear naturally?
checkpointAnswers: n-hat carries the oriented imaginary axis of the rotor.
n-hat belongs to the S^3 rotor coordinate, while s is a real logarithmic scale coordinate.
Unit quaternions and SU(2) describe the same compact group structure through complex matrices.
chapters.finalTakeaway: The rotation form gives angle and oriented axis on S^3 without confusing either with the independent scale coordinate.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Standard quaternionic and Lie-group background used as QSG coordinate vocabulary.

sections.title: 4pi rotation identity and topology of spin
Geodesics, tangent spaces, and Hopf fibration
Rotation as movement along great circles of S^3

chapters.title: Differential Geometry on S^3
chapters.subtitle: Manifold structure and metrics
objectives: Describe S^3 as a smooth manifold and Lie group.
Use invariant vector fields and metrics on SU(2).
Relate curvature and volume to the hypersphere model.
Use the S^3 Laplacian as a bridge to spectra.
checkpointPrompts: Why does QSG start from S^3 before S^3 x R?
What operator first connects geometry to spectra?
checkpointAnswers: S^3 is compact and standard enough to support cleaner definitions first.
The Laplacian or a Dirac-type operator turns geometry into spectral data.
chapters.finalTakeaway: Differential geometry supplies the motion and variation tools needed before spectral operators are introduced.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Standard S^3/SU(2) geometry used as the compact QSG base.

sections.title: SU(2) Lie group structure and invariant vector fields
Connection forms, curvature, and volume measure

chapters.title: Spectral Calculus
chapters.subtitle: Operators, eigenvalues, scale, and resonance
objectives: State operators, spectra, and functional calculus as QSG tools.
Use Rayleigh quotients and variational language cautiously.
Connect spectral modes to geometry without overclaiming physical interpretation.
Separate the continuous scale coordinate from discrete localized resonance modes.
Prepare the algebra, representation, and commutator checks.
checkpointPrompts: What must be specified before an operator is meaningful?
What does a commutator test?
checkpointAnswers: The domain or finite substitute, state space, algebra action, metric, and intended spectral behavior.
It tests how the operator detects variation in an observable.
chapters.finalTakeaway: Spectral calculus is where scale geometry acquires operators and possible localized resonance modes.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Definitions and candidate operators, not completed spectral-triple proofs.

sections.title: Spectra of operators on SU(2) x R

chapters.title: Spectral Theory on S^3 x R
chapters.subtitle: Hypersphere harmonics and scale modes
objectives: Separate compact S^3 prototypes from scale-anchored S^3 x R models.
Use harmonic analysis on SU(2) as a spectral vocabulary.
Distinguish S^3 harmonic labels from scale-mode labels.
Track when compact-resolvent claims are available and when they are not.
State the theorem target for a spectral-triple-compatible model.
checkpointPrompts: Why is compact S^3 easier than S^3 x R?
How do S^3 harmonics differ from scale modes?
What must be checked for a spectral triple?
checkpointAnswers: S^3 is compact, while S^3 x R is noncompact unless the s-direction is controlled.
S^3 harmonics describe orientation-space structure, while scale modes describe localization along s.
One must specify A, H, D, representation, self-adjointness/domain, compact resolvent or finite analogue, and bounded commutators.
chapters.finalTakeaway: The compact prototype is the honest first target; the scale-anchored model is a later controlled extension.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Prototype spectral geometry with explicit unresolved analytic checks.

sections.title: Fourier and Laplace transforms on SU(2) x R
Scale, energy, frequency, and orientation in spectral space

chapters.title: The Anchor-Generating Quaternionic Factorial (AGQF)
chapters.subtitle: Scale-localized resonance wells and conjectural quantization
objectives: Treat AGQF as an extension after the scale and spectral stages are in view.
Interpret anchor forms as candidate scale-localized resonance wells.
Map a well center s_n to a selected scale rho_n = rho_* exp(s_n).
Separate definitions and examples from conjectural quantization language.
Avoid presenting AGQF as part of the minimum spectral-triple core.
checkpointPrompts: What is AGQF's status in the course?
Why does the scale coordinate matter here?
What does s -> -s imply for an s^2 well profile?
checkpointAnswers: It is a conjectural extension and teaching lens, not a proved core axiom.
It supplies the continuous dilation direction where well-like profiles may localize preferred scales.
It pairs reciprocal scales around rho_*, a symmetry that must be stated as intentional or treated as a profile artifact.
chapters.finalTakeaway: AGQF is a conjectural mechanism for localizing modes in scale space, downstream of the root coordinate and operator model.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Conjectural extension; not a completed quantization theorem or established physical dimension.

sections.title: Anchored AGQF forms on the scale coordinate
Sine-prefactor lattice, reciprocal scales, and zero boundaries
Effective potential and scale-localized resonance wells
Quantization as conjectural confinement in scale space

chapters.title: Quaternionic Spectral Coherence
chapters.subtitle: Phase, orientation, scale localization, and field stability
objectives: Define coherence example by example.
Relate alignment to phase, orientation, scale localization, and resonance structure.
Use coherence metrics as scoped diagnostic tools.
Keep field-stability language tied to explicit models.
formulas: coherence = persistence under a stated action or operator
checkpointPrompts: What makes a coherence claim mathematically inspectable?
What must be avoided?
checkpointAnswers: It states what is coherent, under which action, and how it is measured or bounded.
Avoid implying universal field stability without a specified model.
chapters.finalTakeaway: Coherence is useful only when the conserved or persistent phase, orientation, or scale structure is precisely named.

sections.title: Coherence as alignment of quaternionic phase vectors
Spectral coherence functions and correlation metrics

chapters.title: Special Functions and Operators
chapters.subtitle: The quaternionic factorial and spectral tools
objectives: Collect reusable computational and operator tools.
Separate ordinary operator theory from QSG-specific extensions.
Mark zeta and Euler-product connections as frontier material.
Avoid converting suggestive structure into proof claims.
formulas: operator tools must name A, H, D, and pi before spectral claims
checkpointPrompts: How should zeta-linked material be labeled?
What makes an operator reusable?
checkpointAnswers: As a frontier question unless a precise theorem is proved.
It has a stated domain or finite substitute, action, and computable behavior.
chapters.finalTakeaway: Special tools are useful branches, but the root remains the precise operator model.
chapters.claimScopeNote: Conjectural and frontier material where not backed by explicit theorem statements.

sections.title: Relations to pi, Gamma(1/2), and spectral multipliers
Connections to zeta and Euler products

chapters.title: Computational Quaternionic Geometry
chapters.subtitle: Algorithms, simulation, and visualization
objectives: Represent quaternions and SU(2) rotations numerically.
Represent and visualize the logarithmic scale coordinate.
Use finite truncations and visualizations as examples, not proofs.
Track which structures survive discretization.
Connect computational models back to canonical roots.
formulas: finite truncation approximates the chosen infinite model only after stated choices
checkpointPrompts: Why are finite models useful?
What must a visualization trace back to?
checkpointAnswers: They make spectra, commutators, scale profiles, and examples computable while replacing some analytic requirements.
A canonical root such as S^3, SU(2), the scale coordinate, matrix bridge, or spectral operator.
chapters.finalTakeaway: Computation is a branch that tests and visualizes the roots; it is not the canonical root itself.

sections.title: Numerical representation of quaternions and SU(2)
Visualization of scale wells, isosurfaces, and coherence fields
Data structures for quaternionic tensors and signals

chapters.title: Applications and Frontiers
chapters.subtitle: From theory to technology
objectives: Connect QSG to applications without implying completed physics.
Name open problems as open problems.
Trace each application back to roots in coordinates, operators, or spectra.
Keep the spectral-triple question visible as the mathematical test.
formulas: application lens = root model + stated interpretation + scoped evidence
checkpointPrompts: What is the safest first mathematical ask?
How should frontier material be framed?
checkpointAnswers: Whether the compact S^3 prototype can be made into an honest spectral triple and whether the scale extension adds nonredundant structure.
As open research direction, not settled theory.
chapters.finalTakeaway: QSG applications are branches; the trunk is the canonical orientation, scale, and operator framework.

sections.title: Quaternionic signal processing and communication systems
Orientation tracking in robotics and navigation
Scale-aware quantum spectral interpretation of atomic systems
Open problems: scale spectra, spectral packing, prime structure, and zeta fields

appendices: A: Quaternion algebra identities and proofs
B: Volume, curvature, and conformal-cylinder formulas on S^3 x R
D: Reference tables of hypersphere harmonics, scale modes, and constants
